      Node: HEARTBEAT — NETWORK PARTITION

      (HB timeout, worker alive)

      I. Rational Understanding — What Are We Really Observing?

      We observe a split between local activity and global observability:

      • Worker process calls heartbeat locally (so app-level work continues)
      • Temporal server shows no heartbeat events (so scheduler believes the activity stalled).
      • Network telemetry (latency, packet loss, resets) indicates connectivity issues between worker and Temporal.
      • Multiple workers affected simultaneously often points to an infra/network plane problem, not an app bug.
      The application is but the control plane can’t see it. The system’s concept of liveness (heartbeat received) is disconnected from the worker’s perception of being alive. This mismatch drives false timeouts, unsafe retries, and possible duplicate side-effects.
      • If local heartbeats are being invoked but Temporal receives none, assume a network-layer partition (or a transport-level failure) until proven otherwise. Treat it as an infrastructure incident that can cause coordinated false-worker death events, cascading retries, and workload misdirection.

      III. Critical Rational Points

      1. Observation lag is dangerous — by the time Temporal declares heartbeat missing, many heartbeats may have been lost; quick mitigations required.
      2. Temporary increases in heartbeat_timeout are pragmatic — they reduce false positives, but they also increase detection latency for true crashes. Choose policy by risk (safety vs progress).
      3. Multiple workers affected → systemic fault — don’t treat it as isolated probability; assume shared network path or control-plane degradation.
      4. DNS/TLS issues can mimic partition — failure modes include name resolution, certificate expiry, proxy auth failure.
      5. Autoscalers & failover logic can worsen the problem — autoscalers may spawn pods in the same network island, increasing load without restoring connectivity.
      6. Instrumentation blindness — adding heartbeat logging locally is necessary but insufficient; synthetic end-to-end probes are required.

      IV. Detection & Triage — Key Checks (beyond the checklist)

      • Confirm local heartbeat invocation: inspect worker logs showing heartbeat call timestamps and payload success.
      • Confirm Temporal side: check Temporal history for last heartbeat event timestamp for that activity.
      • Investigate host-level connectivity: ping, curl/gRPC connect, DNS resolution times.
      • Correlate across fleet: are many workers experiencing the same symptoms? which AZ / Node pool / subnet?
      • Check intermediary infra: LB health, gateway NAT, egress NAT IP stickiness, firewall/security-group change windows.
      • Check mutual TLS logs: handshake failures, certificate mismatches, expired certs.

      Deeper questions to ask immediately:

      • Are the affected workers collocated on same subnet/AZ/node image?
      • Did any infra change (deploy, config push) happen within the last N minutes?
      • Are Temporal server endpoints overloaded or misbehaving (5xx, connection throttling)?
      • Are these heartbeats lost in flight or dropped by the server (e.g., rate-limiter)?
      • What’s the p99 network latency between worker sidecar and Temporal for the last 10 minutes?

      V. Scoring Confidence (for automation decisions)

      Use a composite confidence score to decide whether to act automatically or escalate:

      def network_partition_score(local_heartbeat_log, temporal_history, net_metrics):    score = 0.0
          if local_heartbeat_log.most_recent_within(60): score += 0.35
          if not temporal_history.has_heartbeat_within(2*hb_interval): score += 0.25
          if net_metrics.packet_loss > 1.0: score += 0.2
          if net_metrics.p99_latency > latency_threshold: score += 0.1
          if tls_errors_detected(): score += 0.1
          return score  # 0..1
      • If score >= 0.7 → treat as high-confidence partition and run automated partition-mitigation steps.
      • If 0.4 <= score < 0.7 → : collect artifacts and escalate to SRE.
      • If < 0.4 → monitor, but avoid disruptive mitigation.
      Scores allow automation to be conservative when evidence is incomplete.

      VI. Immediate Actions — Prioritized & Safe

      Actions organized by speed and impact. Always prefer first, then controlled mitigations.

      1. Verify network path (fast, low-risk)

      • curl -v https://<temporal-host>:7233 (expect gRPC / 200-series connection-level response)
      • tcptraceroute <temporal-host> 7233 or telnet <temporal-host> 7233
      • openssl s_client -connect <host>:7233 -servername <host> to test TLS handshake

      Check DNS caches, local resolvers, and egress NAT IP.

      If curl/tcp connect fails: record connectivity_failed.

      2. Increase heartbeat timeout (fast mitigant — buy time)

      • Deploy a short-lived config change increasing heartbeat_timeout × 2–3 for affected activity types or worker pools. Prioritize this over heavy infra changes because it prevents false timeouts immediately.
      # apply temporarilyset_heartbeat_timeout(activity_type, current * 3)

      increases detection latency for true crashes. Use short TTL (e.g., revert in 30–60 minutes after fix).

      3. Synthetic probe / verification (low-impact, high-signal)

      • From a control plane (or probe pod) perform repeated gRPC connect+heartbeat publish attempts to Temporal endpoint to measure in-band success rate.
      def synthetic_probe(target):    for i in range(5):        except Exception as e: record(e)

      4. Worker placement & routing (conservative)

      • If the partition affects a single AZ/subnet: proactively cordon node(s) and drain workers to healthy zones. Use node affinity to schedule replacements in healthy AZs.
      • If multi-AZ failure: avoid mass movement; prefer short lived heartbeat timeout and infra fix.

      5. Emergency fallback (higher cost)

      • If Temporal endpoint in region is known unhealthy and failover plan exists: switch worker clients to alternate Temporal endpoint (failover cluster). Must ensure cross-region data consistency before failback.

      Prefer config/time-limited mitigations first (heartbeat timeout) and only escalate to routing/failover if necessary.

      Automated Partition Response Orchestrator

      def handle_hb_partition_event(event):    score = network_partition_score(event.local_log, event.temporal_history, event.net_metrics)        log("Low confidence; continue monitoring")    if score >= 0.7:
              # High confidence: run automatic mitigations
              increase_heartbeat_timeout(event.activity_type, factor=3, ttl_minutes=60)
              start_synthetic_probes(event.temporal_host)
              if probes_fail_consistently():
                  # If partition persists and many workers affected
                  if is_zone_specific(event.affected_nodes):
                      cordon_and_drain_nodes(event.affected_nodes)
                      reschedule_workers_to(healthy_zone)                if can_failover_temporal():
                          switch_client_endpoints(event.worker_pool, backup_temporal_endpoint)
                      else:
                          page_oncall("Network partition persists; manual mitigation required")
          else:
              # Ambiguous: collect artifacts and escalate
              collect_artifacts(event)
              page_oncall_with_context(event)
      any automated change must be TTL-limited (e.g., heartbeat timeout revert enforced).

      VIII. Verification — Prove Recovery

      • Confirm curl / gRPC connect works from previously affected pod.
      • Temporal history shows new heartbeat events for previously stalled activities.
      • No new heartbeat timeout alerts in the next heartbeat window.
      • Monitor retries and duplicate side-effect indicators for a period equal to STC + safety buffer.
      def verify_partition_recovery(worker, temporal_host):    if can_connect(worker, temporal_host):
              if temporal_history.shows_heartbeat(worker.activity):
                  log("Recovery verified")
                  schedule_timeout_revert(activity_type)
                  return True
          alert("Recovery validation failed")
          return False
      Verification must be time-bounded and correlate both client and server perspectives.

      Investigate in parallel across layers:

      • Timeline correlation: did firewall/security-group changes or route table changes precede event?
      • Packet captures: tcpdump on worker node and on border interfaces to see SYN/ACK behavior, RSTs, retransmissions. Look for dropped packets, out-of-order delivery, long retransmit windows.
      • NAT gateway saturation: check SNAT connection table exhaustion.
      • LB health: check backend health, 5xx rates, downstream errors.
      • DNS TTL anomalies or DNS servers unreachable -> cause name resolution failures.
      • TLS handshake failures: expired certificate, SNI mismatch, TLS version mismatch, cipher suite negotiation failure.
      • Frontend/thrift/gRPC logs for connection limits, authentication failures, rate limiting, or resource saturation.
      • Database or history-service latency that makes the frontend drop or delay connection handling.
      • gRPC keepalive/timeout: are keepalive intervals too long, or keepalive disabled?
      • Connection pooling issues: exhausted connection pool leading to local connection queuing.
      • Thread-pool starvation in worker process causing inability to process gRPC client events (rare but possible).
      def rca_partition(event):    correlate_deploy_changes(event.timestamp)
          examine_firewall_rules(event.affected_subnets)
          analyze_tcpdumps(worker_node, border_node)
          inspect_tls_errors(worker_logs, server_logs)
          check_temporal_frontend_metrics()
          return construct_root_cause_report()

      X. Long-Term Remediation & Hardening

      1. Network resilience & architecture

      • Multi-AZ placement for workers and Temporal endpoints.
      • Redundant network paths and active health-aware routing.
      • Use stateful egress gateways with scaling and SNAT pool sizing to prevent connection exhaustion.
      • Prefer regional VPC peering over single NAT gateway for heavy bi-directional traffic.

      2. Heartbeat configuration optimization (formalized)

      Define heartbeat timeout using measurable quantities:

      heartbeat_timeout = (heartbeat_interval × k) + network_latency_margin 
      + processing_jitter_margin
      • k = safety factor (typical 2–4)
      • network_latency_margin = measured p99/95 worker→temporal latency
      • processing_jitter_margin = expected processing/GC jitter

      Example: hb_interval=20s, p99_latency=0.5s, k=3 => hb_timeout >= 3*20 + 0.5 = 60.5s (round up).

      • Use gRPC keepalive and TCP keepalive tuned to expected infra delays.
      • Proper connection pooling, circuit-breakers for gRPC calls, and exponential backoff with jitter.
      • Client-side retry throttling to avoid retry storms during transient partitions.

      4. Observability & synthetic checks

      • Active synthetic probes: worker → temporal connect checks from multiple AZs every N seconds.
      • Monitor connect success rate and rpc roundtrip time metrics; alert when success rate drops below threshold for >X minutes.
      • Correlate network events with application-level alarms automatically.

      5. Automated failover & graceful degradation

      • Maintain secondary Temporal endpoints for read/write failover with controlled failback.
      • Implement “graceful degrade” modes in workers (accept only critical tasks) if connectivity to Temporal is degraded.

      XI. Important Operational Considerations & Invariants

      • TTL for emergency changes: any increase to heartbeat_timeout or endpoint switch must have a TTL and automatic revert to avoid latent configuration creep.
      • : cross-region failover must ensure data consistency (duplicate detection, idempotency).
      • : producers should be token-limited so partitions do not cause unbounded queue growth.
      • : define score thresholds above which automation acts and below which SRE is paged.
      • : preserve tcpdumps, logs, and probe outputs for at least 7 days for RCA.

      XII. Race Conditions & Timing Traps

      • Heartbeat TTL vs partition heal: if you increase hb_timeout too much, real crashes will be detected late. Balance is vital.
      • : scaling up workers during partition merely increases number of clients failing to reach Temporal and can exhaust egress/NAT tables. Block autoscaling on confidence of partition.
      • : automated endpoint switching back-and-forth can create oscillation; implement hysteresis and cooldown.
      • : synthetic probes must be low-volume to avoid adding load during fragile periods.

      XIII. Probing Questions — Critical

      • Is the partition transient (seconds) or persistent (minutes/hours)? How do we measure that programmatically?
      • Which infra component (NAT, LB, peering, DNS) has the highest correlation with failure windows?
      • Are we seeing asymmetric connectivity (worker → Temporal fails but Temporal → worker ok)?
      • Does the Temporal service have backpressure or rate-limiting enabled that treats too many connections as abuse?
      • Are certificate rotations automated and tested for zero-downtime?
      • How does the system behave under partial partition: some workers can connect, others cannot — does that create split-brain scheduling?
      If workers can see themselves as alive but the control plane cannot, who is the authority of truth? How do you reconcile two competing models of liveness?

      XIV. Example — Real-World Incident Walkthrough

      At 11:02 UTC, heartbeats for multiple payment-processor workers stop appearing in Temporal. Workers log local heartbeat calls every 20s; Temporal shows last heartbeat 11:01:05. Synthetic probes from the control plane show 30% packet loss to Temporal host. DNS resolvers return stale CNAME for 2 minutes after a config push.
      1. Automated detector computes score = 0.82 → high-confidence partition.
      2. System increases heartbeat_timeout ×3 for payment-processor with 60min TTL.
      3. Synthetic probe continues failing → SRE cordons affected node pool (AZ A) and drains workloads to AZ B.
      4. NAT gateway metrics show connection table exhausted; ops scale NAT pool and rebalance egress.
      5. Connectivity restored; heartbeats reappear. System reverts heartbeat_timeout after 45m. RCA attributes root cause to a broken config deployment affecting NAT rules.
      No false retries, no double-charges, recovery within 50 minutes. Postmortem: add NAT connection alerts and synthetic probes to detect earlier.

      XV. Final Rational Synthesis

      • Network partitions transform honest workers into  — active locally but invisible to the control plane. The safe response is a combination of fast, low-risk mitigations (increase heartbeat_timeout, synthetic probes) and (restore connectivity, scale egress). Automation must be evidence-driven, TTL-bound, and conservative — because wrong automated routing or scaling during a partition can multiply the failure.
      If the system can’t see the worker’s heartbeats, the safest first step is to buy time (short, reversible heartbeat timeout increase), measure (synthetic probes + tcpdump), and act only when confidence is high — prioritizing containment and auditability above speed.

      NODE: SCHEDULE-TO-CLOSE EXCEEDED

      (Total SLA breach — permanent)

      I. Rational Understanding — What Are We Really Observing?

      Schedule-to-Close (STCLOSE) exceeded is the system-level declaration that automated attempts to finish an activity (initial run + retries + backoffs) have failed within the workflow’s allowed lifetime. This is not a transient failure — it’s the system signaling that (retry/backoff/timeout) has been exhausted and or compensating logic must decide between failure, compensation, or risky extension.

      This node sits at the : technical telemetry meets legal/financial/business invariants. Actions here change customer money, obligations, or safety.

      The system tells you “we could not fix this automatically.” The correct response must therefore be principled, auditable and reversible where possible.

      If STCLOSE is exceeded, treat the activity as terminal by default.

      Any deviation (extend, retry, partial commit) requires an explicit, auditable decision that balances: idempotency guarantees, business risk, legal exposure, and technical feasibility of compensation.

      III. Criticality — Why This Is Extremely Sensitive

      • Financial/legal exposure: retried payments, refunds, or double-ships can incur large costs.
      • delayed or lost customer flows degrade reputation.
      • partial state across services (reservations, holds) risks long-term inconsistency.
      • many side-effects are not perfectly compensatable.
      • regulators expect traceable decisions.

      This node demands and .

      IV. Diagnosis Confirmation — What to Verify Immediately

      Before any action, collect and verify the minimal evidentiary set:

      1. timeline of attempts (timestamps), retry counts, last error codes, backoff intervals.
      2. sum of queue wait, execution durations, and backoff windows.
      3. which external systems were touched? (payments, DB writes, external APIs) — capture request IDs, transaction IDs, and partial commit markers.
      4. whether activity or external systems support idempotency tokens or dedupe IDs.
      5. map workflow → business operation (customer id, order id, amount, SLA class).
      6. configured schedule_to_close_timeout, per-activity retry policy, and global fail-open/fail-closed flags.

      Minimal evidence for any irreversible action: workflow_id, activity_name, last_error, retry_count, side_effects_list, account/tenant_id, timestamp_range.

      V. Decision Matrix — Fail vs Compensate vs Extend

      Use this decision matrix (programmatic logic) as the landing policy. It returns a and .

      def recommend_action(activity_snapshot):    # inputs: activity_snapshot contains retry_count, side_effects, idempotency_level, business_risk_score
          if activity_snapshot.side_effects == False:
              return ("FAIL_GRACEFULLY", confidence=0.95)
          if activity_snapshot.idempotency == "idempotent" and activity_snapshot.business_risk < RISK_THRESHOLD:
              return ("AUTO_SAFE_RETRY_ONCE", confidence=0.8)
          if can_compensate(activity_snapshot) and activity_snapshot.business_risk >= RISK_THRESHOLD:
              return ("COMPENSATE_AND_FAIL", confidence=0.9)
          # fallback to manual triage
          return ("MANUAL_TRIAGE", confidence=0.6)
      • FAIL_GRACEFULLY: mark workflow failed, emit audit log, notify owners.
      • AUTO_SAFE_RETRY_ONCE: single controlled retry with extended timeout and strict guard rails.
      • COMPENSATE_AND_FAIL: execute a compensating workflow, then mark terminal.
      • MANUAL_TRIAGE: page human on-call; do not perform further automation.
      Who sets RISK_THRESHOLD? — product + legal + finance; must be explicit per activity class.

      VI. Immediate Actions — Prioritized (human + automation)

      Follow this ordered plan; each step is auditable and reversible where possible.

      1) Business Impact Triage (first 2 minutes)

      • Identify impacted entities and severity: number of customers, dollar value, time-to-customer SLA.
      • Decide communication class: internal only / CS notification / public communication.
      • Create incident ticket with all evidence snapshot.

      2) Snapshot & Lock (evidence preservation)

      • Snapshot workflow state and persist to durable store (S3/DB).
      • Lock activity from automated retries or any automation that may change external side effects.

      3) Determine default automated path via DecisionRecommendation.

      • If FAIL_GRACEFULLY → take automated graceful failure (see templates below).
      • If AUTO_SAFE_RETRY_ONCE → perform a single guarded retry.
      • If COMPENSATE_AND_FAIL → run compensation flow (automated or semi-automated).
      • If MANUAL_TRIAGE → page humans and await instruction.

      4) Business communication

      • Notify CS/Finance if customer-impacting financials involved. Use the ticketing channel with required fields.
      • Emit a structured audit event with decision rationale (who/what/why), and persist for compliance.

      Orchestration of Immediate Response

      def handle_stclose_exceeded(event):    snapshot = snapshot_activity(event.activity_id)
          decision, confidence = recommend_action(snapshot)
          record_audit(event, snapshot, decision, confidence)
          lock_activity_from_automation(snapshot)
          if decision == "FAIL_GRACEFULLY":
              execute_fail_gracefully(snapshot)
              notify_stakeholders(snapshot, reason="STCLOSE exceeded - graceful fail")
          elif decision == "AUTO_SAFE_RETRY_ONCE":
              perform_guarded_retry(snapshot)
          elif decision == "COMPENSATE_AND_FAIL":
              run_compensation(snapshot)
              execute_fail_gracefully(snapshot)        page_oncall_with_context(snapshot)
              wait_for_human_decision(snapshot)
      every automated step must be idempotent; all changes logged; all retries single-shot and reversible where possible.

      VIII. Templates — Actions & Rollbacks

      A) Fail Gracefully — Template

      def execute_fail_gracefully(snapshot):    workflow.fail(reason="ScheduleToCloseExceeded", details=snapshot.summary)
          emit_metric("stclose.failed", labels=snapshot.labels)
          create_postmortem_ticket(snapshot)
      Recording failure is final; rollback involves manual remediation and possible compensations.

      B) Guarded Single Retry — Template

      def perform_guarded_retry(snapshot):    set_activity_timeout(snapshot, multiplier=2)
          run_one_attempt(snapshot.activity, idempotency_token=gen_token())
          if success:    else:        page_oncall_with_context(snapshot)
      If this retry produces harmful side-effects, the compaction path or manual compensate must run.
      def run_compensation(snapshot):    comp = build_compensating_activity(snapshot)
          submit_and_wait(comp, timeout=comp_timeout)
          if comp.succeeded:
              mark_compensated(snapshot)
          else:
              page_oncall("Compensation failed", context=snapshot)
      Compensation itself must be idempotent and audited; if compensation fails, escalate to manual.

      IX. Verification — Prove the Outcome

      After decision execution, verify these postconditions:

      • Workflow terminal state reached and recorded.
      • For compensations: external system invariants restored (e.g., account balance, inventory availability).
      • No orphaned reservations/locks exist.
      • Customer notifications logged and sent if required.
      • Root snapshot & audit trail persisted.
      def verify_postcondition(snapshot):    assert workflow_terminal(snapshot.workflow_id)
          if snapshot.side_effects:
              assert external_systems_consistent(snapshot)
          log_verification(snapshot)

      Do not close incident until verification passes.

      X. Root Cause Analysis — Structured RCA Template

      RCA must be systematic and produce actionable remediation:

      1) Timeline decomposition (timestamp-accurate)

      • total_elapsed = t_end - t_start
      • Compute: sum_queue_wait, sum_execution_time, sum_backoff_time
      • Identify which bucket consumed the majority: dominant_phase = argmax([...])
      • Was schedule_to_close_timeout computed against realistic inputs? Recompute using formula (below).
      • Are initial_interval, backoff_coefficient, max_interval, max_attempts reasonable given dependency SLAs?

      4) Infrastructure contribution

      • Were queue saturation, STS starvation, network partitions, or DB slowdowns present?
      • Long execution paths, unoptimized I/O, blocking calls, unbounded loops.
      • Did the workflow touch high-risk operations? Should we change fail policy for such activities?

      RCA must output: root_cause_tag, remediation_actions, SLAs changed? (yes/no), suggested tests.

      XI. Formula — How to compute a defensible STCLOSE

      Use a conservative formula that captures expected work + retries + queue latency margin:

      STCLOSE = (expected_duration × safety_factor)           + (expected_retries × avg_backoff_seconds) 
                + queue_latency_margin_seconds 
                + processing_jitter_margin_seconds

      Example calculation (digit-by-digit careful):

      • expected_duration = 10 minutes → 10 × 60 = 600 seconds
      • safety_factor = 1.5 → 600 × 1.5 = 900 seconds
      • avg_backoff_seconds = assume geometric backoff 2s,4s,8s average = (2+4+8)/3 = 4.666… → but better to compute sum = 2+4+8 = 14 seconds
      • queue_latency_p99 = 30 seconds
      • jitter_margin = 30 seconds

      Sum STCLOSE = 900 + 14 + 30 + 30 = 974 seconds → round up to 16.5 minutes (≈16m 14s). Prefer to set to 17 minutes in config.

      use sums of actual backoff sums rather than averages to avoid under-provisioning.

      XII. Long-Term Remediations & Policies

      1. break large monolith activities into smaller sub-activities (validate → act → commit).
      2. Idempotency & side-effect model: enforce idempotency tokens; classify activities by side-effect risk (low/medium/high).
      3. maintain tested, versioned compensation workflows for critical actions (payments, inventory, legal operations).
      4. Retry budgets & early warning: surface per-activity retry exhaustion rate on dashboards; alert when >X exhausted in Y minutes.
      5. Adaptive STCLOSE calculation: compute STCLOSE from historical percentiles (p95 execution × safety factor + backoffs + queue p99). Recompute monthly.
      6. build an audited “extend once” UI for engineers/product owners to extend STCLOSE for a workflow with mandatory reason, TTL, and rollback plan.
      7. include STCLOSE-exceeded scenarios in integration & chaos tests to validate compensation correctness and human workflow.
      8. per-activity policy: fail-default, fail-closed, auto-retry-once-with-guard, require-compensation. This registry is the source-of-truth for decisions.

      XIII. Questions to Probe (operational, business, and meta)

      • Which phase consumed most of the STCLOSE budget? (queue vs execution vs backoff)
      • Are retries hiding persistent dependency outages?
      • Were there correlated infrastructure anomalies (DB latency, network spikes) in the same window?
      • What is the financial exposure per failed workflow? Total exposure across all affected workflows?
      • Is there regulatory obligation to notify users or authorities?
      • Who has authority to extend STCLOSE in production — SRE, product, legal? How is approval captured?
      • How much automation do we allow for activities that touch money or personal data?
      Do our SLAs reflect realistic system behavior, or do they reflect optimistic engineering assumptions?

      XIV. Edge Cases & Race Conditions

      • Back-to-back extension race: two humans extend STCLOSE simultaneously — enforce single-source-of-truth and write-once ticketing.
      • Retry storms after extension: extending timeouts without addressing root cause may allow a retry storm to run longer; ensure backoff caps and rate limits.
      • Compensation ordering hazard: compensating out of order can create inconsistent state across systems — impose strict compensation sequencing.
      • ensure all extension decisions are immutably logged (tamper-evident).
      def handle_stclose_event(event):    snapshot = snapshot_activity_state(event.activity_id)
          impact = assess_business_impact(snapshot)
          decision, confidence = recommend_action(snapshot)
          record_audit_event(snapshot, decision, confidence, impact)
          lock_activity(snapshot.activity_id)  # prevents further automation
          if decision == "FAIL_GRACEFULLY":
              execute_fail_gracefully(snapshot)
          elif decision == "AUTO_SAFE_RETRY_ONCE":
              perform_guarded_retry(snapshot)
          elif decision == "COMPENSATE_AND_FAIL":
              run_compensation(snapshot)
              execute_fail_gracefully(snapshot)
          else:
              # require explicit human approval
              page_oncall(snapshot)
              await human_decision(snapshot, timeout=human_timeout)
              # run action selected by human
          verify_postcondition(snapshot)
          create_rca_task(snapshot)

      XVI. Final Rational Synthesis

      Schedule-to-Close exceeded is the that automation cannot reconcile progress with risk. Treat it with gravity: preserve evidence, lock automation, follow a rigorous decision model that mixes automation for safe, low-risk cases and human judgment for ambiguous/high-risk cases. Build defenses (idempotency, compensation library, decomposition) so fewer flows reach this node. When they do, the decision must be auditable, reversible where possible, and informed by a tight RCA that updates policy and prevents recurrence.

      STCLOSE is not just a timeout — it is a governance event. Design your system, processes, and controls so that every path from STCLOSE leads to a known, testable outcome: fail safely, compensate correctly, or extend only with intentional, logged authority.

      NODE: NON-RETRYABLE ERROR

      (Logic bug or deliberate failure)

      I. Rational Understanding — What Are We Really Observing?

      • A is a semantic termination signal.
      • It’s not a crash, not a timeout — it’s the code itself choosing to abort because it believes repetition would be meaningless or dangerous.

      At this node, the system has reached epistemic certainty: it knows the cause, believes it cannot change, and encodes that as truth via non_retryable=True.

      But truth here is fragile — often it’s not the system that’s certain,it’s the fossilized into configuration.

      This node, therefore, isn’t just a logic problem — it’s an inquiry into in distributed execution.

      Our role is to test whether that certainty is justified.

      If an activity fails with ApplicationError(non_retryable=True), the workflow must retry automatically — but must still determine this classification occurred and whether it reflects truth or mistake.

      A non-retryable failure may stem from:

      1.  — wrong or malformed data.
      2.  — legitimate rule breach.
      3.  — transient failure mislabeled non-retryable.
      4.  — unknown retryable issue under a false flag.

      The system’s job here is to and decide whether to respect, override, or question it.

      III.A System That Questions Certainty

      At this node, the control logic behaves epistemically, not reactively: before accepting a non-retryable result, it must re-validate whether retry suppression is justified.

      Epistemic Challenge Routine

      def verify_non_retryable_certainty(event):    # Step 1: Confirm semantic correctness
          if not event.error.non_retryable:
              return "RETRYABLE_MISFLAG"
          if event.error.code in ["TimeoutError", "ConnectionError"]:
              return "LIKELY_MISCLASSIFIED"
          # Step 2: Infer origin from history
          if event.history.retries == 0:
              return "FIRST_ATTEMPT_FATAL"
          if event.history.has_identical_inputs():
              return "DETERMINISTIC_FAILURE"
          # Step 3: Look for contradictions
          if event.context.network_glitch_recent():    return "CONFIRMED_NON_RETRYABLE"

      The system must not trust the non_retryable flag blindly — it must infer whether the classification itself was correct.

      If certainty is just a flag, who verifies the flagger?

      IV. Rational Diagnosis — Expanding the Thought Space

      Invalid data slipped past validation. The fault is epistemic optimism — the system assumed clean data.

      2. Business Logic Failure: The logic enforces a domain constraint (e.g., cannot refund twice). Correct in policy, but final in flow.

      A transient or dependency error mislabeled non-retryable. Dangerous — masks recoverable states.

      A branch raises ApplicationError(non_retryable=True) due to missing context — false finality.

      Each path requires a distinct reasoning pattern.

      V. Immediate Actions — Rational Control Chain

      Algorithm 2 — Non-Retryable Control Logic

      def handle_non_retryable(event):    certainty = verify_non_retryable_certainty(event)
          log("Certainty classification", certainty)
          if certainty == "LIKELY_MISCLASSIFIED":
              quarantine_activity(event)        return
          if certainty == "MAYBE_TRANSIENT":
              if event.history.retries == 0:
                  schedule_safe_one_retry(event)
              else:
                  alert_team("Potential misclassification detected")
              return
          if certainty == "FIRST_ATTEMPT_FATAL":
              analyze_input_payload(event)
          if certainty == "DETERMINISTIC_FAILURE":
              execute_compensation_if_needed(event)
              fail_workflow_gracefully(event)
              return
          if certainty == "CONFIRMED_NON_RETRYABLE":
              finalize_and_audit(event)

      VI. Input and Side Effect Audit

      When the system reaches non-retryable finality, the next rational step is not to fix, but to : confirm that nothing unsafe was left behind.

      Algorithm 3 — Side-Effect Audit

      III. Scoring & Confidence — When to Automate vs Escalate

      Define a composite recovery_confidence_score to drive automated behavior:

      def recovery_confidence(error, history, dependency_metrics):    score = 0.0
          # Freshness: first attempt increases optimism
          if history.attempts == 1:    # Error class: known transient classes increase optimism
          if error.type in TRANSIENT_TYPES:  # e.g., ConnectionTimeout, 503
              score += 0.25

          if history.affected_scope == "single":

          # Backoff consumption: many retries consumed -> pessimistic
          score -= min(0.3, history.retry_ratio)  # retry_ratio = attempts / max_attempts
          return clamp(score, 0.0, 1.0)
      • score >= 0.7 → automation may continue with safeguards.
      • 0.4 <= score < 0.7 → collect artifacts and alert SRE for review.
      • score < 0.4 → pause automatic retries and escalate immediately.

      VI. Retry Policy Evaluation & Adaptive Tuning

      Retry policy must be a living thing — tune it to cost, criticality, and dependency tolerance.

      Baseline backoff timeline calculation (example):

      • attempt 2: +initial_interval
      • attempt 3: +initial_interval × backoff_coefficient
      • attempt 4: +initial_interval × backoff_coefficient²

      Adaptive tuning algorithm:

      def adaptive_retry_policy(event, dependency_metrics):    policy = load_default_policy(event.activity_type)
          if dependency_metrics.error_rate > 0.2:
              # slow down: increase initial interval and cap attempts
              policy.initial_interval *= 2
              policy.max_interval = min(policy.max_interval * 2, timedelta(minutes=10))
              policy.max_attempts = max(3, policy.max_attempts - 2)
          elif dependency_metrics.error_rate < 0.01:
              # speed up recovery for healthy deps
              policy.initial_interval = max(timedelta(seconds=1), policy.initial_interval / 2)
      adaptivity avoids both hammering a failing service and waiting too long for recovery.

      Part IV: Advanced Decision-Making Frameworks

      I. Rational Premise — Decision-Making as Systemic Cognition

      At million-operation scale, failure itself becomes a data structure. Incidents are no longer discrete — they form patterns, correlations, and recursive dependencies across worker fleets, Temporal servers, and external APIs.

      A resilient architecture cannot simply ; it must  — reasoning about uncertainty, cost, and propagation before the next retry fires.

      This section formalizes the of the decision tree — the algorithms that let a distributed system choose what to think about next.

      4.1 Multi-Dimensional Failure Correlation — Seeing the System as a Whole

      I. Core Rational Understanding

      No failure is atomic. Every “timeout” is an echo through multiple layers:

      • A worker crash might look like a network timeout.
      • A network stall might masquerade as a Temporal scheduling delay.
      • A dependency failure might appear as a retry storm.

      The goal: infer causal relationships between concurrent anomalies and assign probabilistic weight to each possible origin.

      II. Algorithmic Correlation Engine

      The system must transform raw metrics into , compute conditional likelihoods, and rank hypotheses dynamically.

      def correlate_failures(observables, priors, likelihood_model):    symptoms = {
              "sts_p99": observables["sts_latency_p99"],
              "stc_p99": observables["stc_latency_p99"],
              "hb_loss": observables["heartbeat_gap_ratio"],

          }    evidence = 0
          for cause, prior in priors.items():
              likelihood = likelihood_model(cause, symptoms)
              posteriors[cause] = prior * likelihood
              evidence += posteriors[cause]        posteriors[cause] /= max(evidence, 1e-12)
          return sorted(posteriors.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

      • Action: Query poller count, inspect worker resource metrics, then branch to node.

      4.4 Cascade Failure Prevention — Containing Entropy

      V. Rational Layer — Why This Is Hard

      • Failures are not binary; they . Containment requires prediction, not observation.

      When do you trust a machine’s judgment of correlation enough to let it act without human supervision?

      VI. Final Rational Synthesis

      Advanced decision-making in distributed systems is not about eliminating uncertainty — it’s about thinking clearly within it.

      The decision tree is not a static flowchart but a dynamic Bayesian control system that weighs:

      • correlation strength (multi-dimensional evidence),
      • cost of cognition (expected decision utility),
      • uncertainty (probabilistic belief updates), and
      • temporal urgency (containment before cascade).

      Every decision node must answer three questions before it acts:

      1. What am I most confident about right now?
      2. What is the cheapest question I can ask next?
      3. If I am wrong, how much damage will I cause before I can roll back?

      The system that answers these faster than it fails — wins.

      Part V: Operational Excellence — Metrics, Alerts & Runbooks

      I. Rational Premise — The Discipline of Knowing When You Know

      Operational Excellence is not a process metric.It is the between what the system believes is happening and what is actually happening.

      A distributed system does not fail silently — it fails .But noise is not knowledge. Noise is the illusion of awareness.

      Every alert you design is not a bell — it’s a question:

      “Do we still understand our own system?”

      5.1 Observability Stack Design — Reconstructing Reality from Shadows

      Observability is not about seeing everything; it’s about seeing the right thing at the right time. Yet, the harder you instrument, the more data you create — and the less you understand.

      You now face the :

      The more you measure, the less you comprehend.

      Because comprehension decays faster than telemetry grows.

      At scale, every millisecond produces tens of thousands of metrics.Each metric claims: But 99% of them describe a world that has already ended.

      Operational truth exists in the 1% of signals that precede the collapse — not in the 99% that describe it after.

      III. The Engineering Question

      How do you know which signals are predictive, not retrospective?How do you measure  — the time between failure and the first meaningful metric that described it?Can your system estimate its own epistemic delay?

      IV. Rational Construction

      Metrics must be designed to , not answers.

      • temporal_task_queue_depth asks: Is there starvation or just high traffic?
      • temporal_activity_timeout_total asks: Is time itself misconfigured?
      • worker_cpu_utilization asks: Is computation trapped in a dead loop or genuine overload?

      Every metric is a question disguised as a number.

      Algorithmic Schema for Signal Selection

      def relevant_signals(metrics):    # Filter metrics that encode causal potential
          return [m for m in metrics if m.changes_before_failure and m.correlation_with_outage > 0.7]
      def observability_signal_score(metric):
          score = (metric.timeliness_weight * metric.lead_time_before_failure) \
                + (metric.information_gain / metric.query_cost)
          return score

      A good metric minimizes and maximizes lead time before irreversibility.

      VI. Complicated Questions of Reality

      • What is the minimum number of signals required to reconstruct a causal failure?

      7.1 Multi-Region Failure Correlation — The Geometry of Uncertainty

      In a multi-region Temporal deployment, no node holds the full truth. Each region perceives only a localized fragment of the total state — a distorted projection delayed by milliseconds, shaped by routing asymmetry, DNS drift, and replication lag.

      Thus, global awareness is not observation; it is inference — a hypothesis continuously invalidated by latency.

      When one region begins to fail, others may appear stable for 30 seconds, 60 seconds, or even 3 minutes. By then, mitigation in one region may have already altered global topology — and every action to “fix” the failure can itself change what the failure means.

      Because timing defines truth.

      • If Region B reports failure before Region A acknowledges success, which report represents reality?
      • At 10 million workflows per hour, each second of delayed correlation equals thousands of false alerts or suppressed warnings.
      • A global reroute, triggered 45 seconds too early, can transform a 3-region partial incident into a 5-region resource storm.

      The cost of premature correctness exceeds the cost of delayed understanding.

      7.3 Formal Verification of Timeout Configurations — The Algebra of Fear

      Time defines the edges of computation. Timeouts are not settings; they are existential statements of how long we are willing to wait before admitting ignorance.

          This is not a design flaw — it is a thermodynamic law of cognition.Information cannot be preserved at the same rate that stability is restored.

          You must lose something: uptime or understanding.

          Thus emerges the real engineering challenge of the next decade:how to design systems that heal themselves without forgetting why they bled.

          VI. The Emergence of Cognitive Reliability

          The future of Temporal systems — and of distributed orchestration as a philosophy — is :

          • Systems that reason about their own uncertainty,
          • That record not just what failed, but why they believed it failed,
          • That leave behind rational trails, not just logs,
          • That slow down automation deliberately to let human cognition catch up.

          Such systems will not measure success in MTTR or SLA percentages, but in  —  the interval between recovery and the moment we can articulate, with confidence,

          This shifts the operational paradigm from:

          “How fast did we fix it?” to

          “How completely did we comprehend it?”

          VII. The Closing Synthesis

          We began with tactical nodes — crash detection, containment, retry exhaustion. We ended with philosophical recursion — machine self-awareness, bounded truth, explainable stability.

          Between those two poles lies the living continuum of modern systems engineering.

          The rational synthesis is this:

          1. To suppress it completely is to silence your teacher.
          2. Automation without understanding is amnesia.Every self-healing mechanism must preserve its reasoning context, or reliability becomes theater.
          3. Resilience is not endurance — it is recollection.A system that remembers its last failure correctly will not fear its next one.
          4. The ultimate SLA is epistemic: 99.999% available 99.999% explainable.

          Because uptime without explanation is not success — it is stagnation disguised as perfection.

          VIII. The Final Question — The Ethics of Certainty

          • If one day our systems never crash, never hang, never alert — will we still understand how they work?
          • When machines begin not only to self-heal but to rewrite their own diagnostic reasoning, when prevention becomes invisible and every anomaly is corrected before we can perceive it — what happens to the human role in knowing?
          • Will we still build systems, or will systems begin building understanding for us?

          The end of failure must not become the end of comprehension. A reliable system that cannot be explained is not progress — it is silence.

          Our task is no longer to build systems that survive chaos, but to build systems that can tell the story of their own survival.

items.title: Configmap Basics
items.author: Techno Freak
items.content:
Kubernetes ConfigMap is a built-in Kubernetes API object

ConfigMap is a type of native designed to store environment-specific data and share it with Pods (which is a Kubernetes component that runs containers and typically hosts applications).ConfigMaps store data as. This means you can define virtually any type of object (the key) and set a corresponding value for it using a .This means you can define virtually any type of object (the key) and set a for it using a ConfigMap.

Why Configmaps are Important ?

ConfigMaps are important because applications often need to access data that can change from one . For instance, as we mentioned above, an application might need to know where it can Since that data is not consistent between one application deployment and another, hard-coding it into container images wouldn’t make a lot of sense. If you did that, you’d have to rebuild your container images every time the configuration data changed.Nor is it easy to inject custom configuration data into Kubernetes applications at runtime using approaches that work in other types of setups, like setting environment variables through the command line or using static configuration settings files. Kubernetes Pods aren’t designed to be easily to these types of resources.

Developing your app to read config values from its environment or filesystem allows you to decouple configuration from code and easily support multiple deployment methods. When you’re working locally on your laptop, you can set straight in your shell; for Kubernetes deployments, you can reference values in a ConfigMap.

With ConfigMaps, however, admins enjoy an easy, flexible way of declaring environment-specific data. They can create ConfigMaps to define application settings at and they can update ConfigMaps whenever those settings change.

How to manage Configmaps ?

To determine which ConfigMaps exist in your cluster, run:

This will generate a list of your ConfigMaps.

To view the contents of a ConfigMap, use the command:

kubectl describe configmaps <ConfigMap name>

This will display the keys and values defined in the ConfigMap you specify as a plaintext file, with keys separated by horizontal lines. You can also pass the option to kubectl if you want it to display the data using YAML.

Sample Example of Configmaps

apiVersion: v1kind: Pod name: my-app-pod containers: image: my-app:latest — name: DB_HOST configMapKeyRef:

name: db-config # Name of the ConfigMap

key: DB_HOST # Key inside the ConfigMap valueFrom: name: db-config — name: DB_USER configMapKeyRef: key: DB_USER valueFrom: name: db-config

Make sure you’ve created the db-config ConfigMap before using it:apiVersion: v1metadata:data:

DB_HOST: my-database.example.com

DB_PORT: “5432” DB_PASSWORD: mypassword

For secrets like passwords, you should ideally use a

Some trade off in configmaps

  • Secrets require careful access control and should be used only for sensitive data.
  • Secrets store data as base64 encoded strings, which can add complexity to access and usage.
  • Updating Secrets can be more challenging than updating ConfigMaps.

Configmap Basics was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Evolution of Envoy AI Gateway - Yan Avlasov, Google & Takeshi Yoneda, Tetrate.io

Envoy AI Gateway is an open source project designed specifically for serving GenAI workloads in the Kubernetes environment. Envoy AI Gateway is rapidly evolving, including features such as:
* Transparent failover between models and model providers,
* Token-based global rate limiting,
* Integration with external load balancing policies that enable efficient utilization of inference resources,
* Improved buffer size management for retries to textual LLMs,
* Support for disaggregated serving in llm-d.

In this talk we will explore use, configuration and inner workings of these new features.

items.title: Lightning Talk: Beyond Lua: Lightweight Envoy Extension With JavaScript - Takeshi Yoneda, Tetrate.io
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Lightning Talk: Beyond Lua: Lightweight Envoy Extension With JavaScript - Takeshi Yoneda, Tetrate.io

With the introduction of dynamic modules in Envoy, it becomes much easier to write high performance custom extensions for Envoy using languages other than C++, which opens up a whole new world of possibilities for Envoy users.
However, the dynamic modules feature itself is low-level and requires a good understanding of Envoy's internals,
especially it must be written in C, C++, Rust or other compiled languages that can be compiled to a shared library.
On the other hand, in practice, platform developers might not be familiar with these languages.

We developed a JavaScript extension built on top of the dynamic modules feature, which allows users to load JavaScript code as an HTTP filter. This is a powerful example of how to use the dynamic modules feature to create high-level extensions in a more accessible language.

In this lightning talk, I will quickly review the dynamic modules feature, and then introduce the JavaScript dynamic module we developed with a live demo.

Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Amsterdam, The Netherlands (23-26 March, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. items.title: Rethinking the Proxy Model: Implementing Envoy as a Node-Scoped Agent - Tony Allen, Google
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Rethinking the Proxy Model: Implementing Envoy as a Node-Scoped Agent - Tony Allen, Google

Sidecar proxies are the most common paradigm for service mesh deployments in Kubernetes, but we're going to explore a radical alternative: deploying Envoy as a node-level proxy instead of a per-pod sidecar.

We'll delve into the motivations for this shift, examining how moving the data plane to the node level addresses the concerns with sidecar deployments. We will also dive deep into the core technical changes in Envoy to make this possible.

The talk will also cover the critical aspect of configuration management in this new model. We'll illustrate how service mesh configurations are represented and applied in a node-level Envoy deployment, contrasting it with the familiar sidecar model.

Finally, we'll present comprehensive resource usage and performance comparisons, demonstrating the tangible benefits in terms of reduced memory footprint, lower CPU consumption, and improved overall throughput that can be achieved by consolidating Envoy instances at the node level.

Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Amsterdam, The Netherlands (23-26 March, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io</article>

items.title: One Size Doesn’t Fit All: Adding Org-Specific Gateway API Extensions wit... Teju Nareddy & Guy Daich
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>One Size Doesn’t Fit All: Adding Org-Specific Gateway API Extensions with Envoy Gateway - Teju Nareddy, Confluent & Guy Daich, SAP

This talk shows how to build an Envoy Gateway extension server, allowing you to define and implement custom GW-API extensions.

When talking about ingress, organizations often face a dilemma: build a bespoke control plane or adopt a CNCF project that may not fully meet their needs. Envoy Gateway offers a hybrid solution with a Kubernetes-native, standardized approach that covers most use cases but allows for customization via control plane extensibility.

Companies like SAP and Confluent, as well as OSS projects like Envoy AI Gateway, use this approach. They enhance Envoy Gateway with custom Gateway-API Policy Attachments and Filter Extensions, enabling custom behaviors in the proxy via xDS—from specific HTTP filters to tailored authorization logic. It’s powerful customization without starting from scratch.

We will explore the extension server's gRPC interface and its relation to Gateway API, and cover operational aspects such as deployment options, failure handling, and security.

Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Amsterdam, The Netherlands (23-26 March, 2026). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. items.title: Envoy From the Inside Out: A Developer's Guide - Akshita Agarwal, Airbnb
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Envoy From the Inside Out: A Developer's Guide - Akshita Agarwal, Airbnb

Ever struggled with debugging Envoy connection issues or considered developing new Envoy filters? This presentation enhances existing Envoy discussions by targeting the developer perspective specifically. We'll examine core components—listeners, clusters, filters, etc through the lens of actual implementation rather than just configuration. You'll follow a connection's complete journey: observing data as it traverses filter chains, understanding the interactions between filter types, and revealing what truly happens when Envoy processes network traffic. Beyond the technical details, you'll gain valuable engineering insights into how Envoy's threading model and memory management techniques enables both high performance and extensibility. You'll leave with practical insights into Envoy's inner workings, empowering you to troubleshoot, enhance, and customize Envoy to suit your organization’s needs.

items.title: Lightning Talk: Rate Limiting With Quotas Is Hard - So We Didn't! - Will Betts & Jay Chauhan, Tesco
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Lightning Talk: Rate Limiting With Quotas Is Hard - So We Didn't! - Will Betts & Jay Chauhan, Tesco

At Tesco, the UK’s biggest supermarket, we run Envoy as a distributed API gateway configured with a purpose-built xDS control plane. We wanted to apply rate limits globally and prototyped a server that implemented Envoy’s RLQS (Rate Limit Quota Service) - a protocol where quotas are distributed dynamically. But API traffic distribution shifts constantly across our Envoy fleet, and we had issues with accuracy as our quota allocation updates lagged behind changes in traffic distribution.

This talk will cover the problems we faced and our evolving design choices as we went from a prototype to a low-overhead and accurate distributed rate limiting solution running in production. During this journey we broke away from RLQS and designed and implemented an alternative protocol that allows us to maintain rate limiting accuracy in the face of rapid traffic redistribution, without extra latency. It also means we need to process orders of magnitude less data in our central rate limiting service!

items.title: Lightning Talk: Flux Unfiltered: Real-World GitOps Lessons From a Four-Part Docuseri... Bart Farrell
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Lightning Talk: Flux Unfiltered: Real-World GitOps Lessons From a Four-Part Docuseries - Bart Farrell, LearnKube

Flux Unfiltered lifts the curtain on the people, pivots, and production issues behind CNCF Flux’s rise from a Weaveworks side-project to a graduated GitOps staple now stewarded by ControlPlane. After recording 8+ hours of candid interviews with founders, maintainers, cloud providers, telcos, banks, and platform vendors, we distilled four recurring themes:

-Why GitOps emerged (and when it doesn’t fit)

-How regulated industries tame Kubernetes drift with Flux

-What happens when commercial backing disappears—and how the CNCF kept the project alive

-Design trade-offs that differentiate Flux and Argo today

In this session we’ll share the insights and findings from the docu-series—surface actionable patterns for teams adopting GitOps at scale. No sales pitch, no product demo—just hard-won lessons and a roadmap of where the community is steering Flux next.

In this session, Stefan will share his experience as a maintainer of Flux CD and how he has seen GitOps evolve over the years. He will start with a brief history of GitOps from the early days of Flux CD to mainstream adoption.

items.title: Ingress NGINX Retirement: What You Need to Know
items.content:
To prioritize the safety and security of the ecosystem, Kubernetes SIG Network and the Security Response Committee are announcing the upcoming retirement of Ingress NGINX. Best-effort maintenance will continue until March 2026. Afterward, there will be no further releases, no bugfixes, and no updates to resolve any security vulnerabilities that may be discovered. Existing deployments of Ingress NGINX will continue to function and installation artifacts will remain available.

We recommend migrating to one of the many alternatives. Consider migrating to Gateway API, the modern replacement for Ingress. If you must continue using Ingress, many alternative Ingress controllers are listed in the Kubernetes documentation. Continue reading for further information about the history and current state of Ingress NGINX, as well as next steps.

Ingress is the original user-friendly way to direct network traffic to workloads running on Kubernetes. (Gateway API is a newer way to achieve many of the same goals.) In order for an Ingress to work in your cluster, there must be an Ingress controller running. There are many Ingress controller choices available, which serve the needs of different users and use cases. Some are cloud-provider specific, while others have more general applicability.

Ingress NGINX was an Ingress controller, developed early in the history of the Kubernetes project as an example implementation of the API. It became very popular due to its tremendous flexibility, breadth of features, and independence from any particular cloud or infrastructure provider. Since those days, many other Ingress controllers have been created within the Kubernetes project by community groups, and by cloud native vendors. Ingress NGINX has continued to be one of the most popular, deployed as part of many hosted Kubernetes platforms and within innumerable independent users’ clusters.

The breadth and flexibility of Ingress NGINX has caused maintenance challenges. Changing expectations about cloud native software have also added complications. What were once considered helpful options have sometimes come to be considered serious security flaws, such as the ability to add arbitrary NGINX configuration directives via the "snippets" annotations. Yesterday’s flexibility has become today’s insurmountable technical debt.

Despite the project’s popularity among users, Ingress NGINX has always struggled with insufficient or barely-sufficient maintainership. For years, the project has had only one or two people doing development work, on their own time, after work hours and on weekends. Last year, the Ingress NGINX maintainers announced their plans to wind down Ingress NGINX and develop a replacement controller together with the Gateway API community. Unfortunately, even that announcement failed to generate additional interest in helping maintain Ingress NGINX or develop InGate to replace it. (InGate development never progressed far enough to create a mature replacement; it will also be retired.)

Current State and Next Steps

Currently, Ingress NGINX is receiving best-effort maintenance. SIG Network and the Security Response Committee have exhausted our efforts to find additional support to make Ingress NGINX sustainable. To prioritize user safety, we must retire the project.

In March 2026, Ingress NGINX maintenance will be halted, and the project will be retired. After that time, there will be no further releases, no bugfixes, and no updates to resolve any security vulnerabilities that may be discovered. The GitHub repositories will be made read-only and left available for reference.

Existing deployments of Ingress NGINX will not be broken. Existing project artifacts such as Helm charts and container images will remain available.

In most cases, you can check whether you use Ingress NGINX by running kubectl get pods \--all-namespaces \--selector app.kubernetes.io/name=ingress-nginx with cluster administrator permissions.

We would like to thank the Ingress NGINX maintainers for their work in creating and maintaining this project–their dedication remains impressive. This Ingress controller has powered billions of requests in datacenters and homelabs all around the world. In a lot of ways, Kubernetes wouldn’t be where it is without Ingress NGINX, and we are grateful for so many years of incredible effort.

SIG Network and the Security Response Committee recommend that all Ingress NGINX users begin migration to Gateway API or another Ingress controller immediately. Many options are listed in the Kubernetes documentation: Gateway API, Ingress. Additional options may be available from vendors you work with.

items.title: Docker Engine v29: Foundational Updates for the Future
items.author: Simeon Ratliff
items.content:
This post is for Linux users running Docker Engine (Community Edition) directly on their hosts. Docker Desktop users don’t need to take any action — Engine updates are included automatically in future Desktop releases.

Docker Engine v29 is a foundational release that sets the stage for the future of the Docker platform. While it may not come with flashy new features, it introduces a few significant under-the-hood changes that simplify our architecture and improve ecosystem alignment:

  • Minimum API version update
  • The Containerd image store is now the default for new installations.
  • Experimental Support for NFTables

These changes improve maintainability, developer experience, and interoperability across the container ecosystem.

Minimum API Version Update

Docker versions older than v25 are now end of life, and as such, we have increased the Minimum API version to 1.44 (Moby v25). 

If you are getting the following error, you will need to update to a newer client or follow the mitigation steps to override the min-version

Override the minimum API version

There are two methods to launch dockerd with a lower minimum API version. Additional information can be found on docs.docker.com

Using flags when starting dockerd

Launch with the set to the previous value. For example:

DOCKER_MIN_API_VERSION=1.24 dockerd

Using a JSON configuration file — daemon.json

Set in your file.

{
  "min-api-version": "1.24"
}

Containerd Image Store Becomes the Default

The Containerd runtime originated as a core component of Docker Engine and was later split out and donated to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). It now serves as the industry-standard container runtime, powering Kubernetes and many other platforms.

While Docker introduced containerd for container execution years ago, we continued using the graph driver storage backend for managing image layers. Meanwhile, containerd evolved its own image content store and snapshotter framework, designed for modularity, performance, and ecosystem alignment.

To ensure stability, Docker has been gradually migrating to the containerd image store over time. Docker Desktop has already used the containerd image store as the default for most of the past year. With Docker Engine v29, this migration takes the next step by becoming the default in the Moby engine.

  • As of Docker Engine v29, the containerd image store becomes the default for image layer and content management for .
  • Legacy graph drivers are still available, but are now deprecated. New installs can still opt out of Containerd image store if there is any issue.
  • : Both execution and storage now use containerd, reducing duplication and internal complexity
  • Unlock new feature possibilities, such as:
    • Lazy pulling of image content
    • Peer-to-peer distribution
  • : Brings Docker Engine in sync with containerd-based platforms, like Kubernetes, improving interoperability.
  • : Enables faster innovation in image layer handling and runtime behaviour

We appreciate that this change may cause some disruption, as the Containerd image store takes a different approach to content and layer management compared to the existing storage drivers.

However, this shift is a positive one. It enables a more consistent, modular, and predictable container experience.

To be clear, these changes only impact new installs; existing users will not be forced to containerd. However, you can start your migration now and opt-in.

We are working on a migration guide to help teams transition and move their existing content to the containerd image store.

  • The graph driver backend will be removed in a future release.
  • Docker will continue evolving the image store experience, leveraging the full capabilities of containerd’s ecosystem.
  • Expect to see enhanced content management, multi-snapshotter support, and faster pull/push workflows in the future.

Moby Migrates to Go Modules

Go modules have been the community standard since 2019, but until now, the Moby project used a legacy vendoring system. Avoiding Go modules was creating:

  • Constant maintenance churn to work around tooling assumptions
  • Confusing workflows for contributors
  • Compatibility issues with newer Go tools and ecosystem practices

Simply put, continuing to resist Go modules was making life harder for everyone.

  • The Moby codebase is now fully module-aware using go.mod.
  • This means cleaner dependency management and better interoperability for tools and contributors.
  • External clients, API libraries, and SDKs will find the Moby codebase easier to consume and integrate with.
  • This is not a user-facing feature—you won’t see a UI or command change.
  • However, it does affect developers who consume Docker’s Go APIs.

Important for Go Developers

If you’re consuming the Docker client or API packages in your own Go projects:

  • The old module path github.com/docker/docker will no longer receive updates.
  • To stay current with Docker Engine releases, you must switch to importing from github.com/moby/moby.

Experimental support for nftables

For bridge and overlay networks on Linux, Docker Engine currently creates firewall rules using “iptables” and “ip6tables”.

In most cases, these commands are linked to “iptables-nft” and “ip6tables-nft”. So, Docker’s rules are translated to nftables behind the scenes.

However, OS distributions are beginning to deprecate support for iptables. It’s past time for Docker Engine to create its own nftables rules directly.

Opt-in support for creating nftables rules instead of iptables.

The rules are functionally equivalent, but there are some differences to be aware of, particularly if you make use of the “DOCKER-USER” chain in iptables.

On a host that uses “firewalld”, iptables rules are created via firewalld’s deprecated “direct” interface. That’s not necessary for nftables because rules are organised into separate tables, each with its own base chains. Docker will still set up firewalld zones and policies for its devices, but it creates nftables rules directly, just as it does on a host without firewalld.

In this initial version, nftables support is “experimental”. Please be cautious about deploying it in a production environment.

Swarm support is planned for a future release. At present, it’s not possible to enable Docker Engine’s nftables support on a node with Swarm enabled.

In a future release, nftables will become the default firewall backend and iptables support will be deprecated.

In addition to adding planned Swarm support, there’s scope for efficiency improvements.

For example, the rules themselves could make more use of nftables features, particularly sets of ports.

These changes will be prioritised based on the feedback received. If you would like to contribute, do let us know!

Getting Started with Engine v29

As mentioned, this post is for Linux users running Docker Engine (Community Edition) directly on their hosts. Docker Desktop users don’t need to take any action — Engine updates are included automatically in the upcoming Desktop releases.

To install Docker Engine on your host or update an existing installation, please follow the guide for your specific OS.

For additional information about this release:

items.title: Connect to Remote MCP Servers with OAuth in Docker
items.author: Simeon Ratliff
items.content:
In just a year, the Model Context Protocol (MCP)has become the standard for connecting AI agents to tools and external systems. The Docker MCP Catalog now hosts hundreds of containerized local MCP servers, enabling developers to quickly experiment and prototype locally.

We have now added support for remote MCP servers to the Docker MCP Catalog. These servers function like local MCP servers but run over the internet, making them easier to access from any environment without the need for local configuration.

With the latest update, the now supports remote MCP servers with OAuth, making it easier than ever to securely connect to external apps like Notion and Linear, right from your Docker environment. Plus, the just grew by 60+ new remote MCP servers, giving you an even wider range of integrations to power your workflows and accelerate how you build, collaborate, and automate.

As remote MCP servers gain popularity, we’re excited to make this capability available to millions of developers building with Docker.

In this post, we’ll explore what this means for developers, why OAuth support is a game-changer, and how you can get started with remote MCP servers with just two simple commands.

Connect to Remote MCP Servers- Securely, Easily, Seamlessly

Goodbye Manual Setup, Hello OAuth Magic

Figuring out how to find and generate API tokens for a service is often tedious, especially for beginners. Tokens also tend to expire unpredictably, breaking existing MCP connections and require reconfiguration.

With OAuth built directly into Docker MCP, you’ll no longer need to juggle tokens or manually configure connections. You can securely connect to remote MCP servers in seconds – all while keeping your credentials safe. 

60+ New Remote MCP Servers, Instantly Available

From project management to documentation and issue tracking, the expanded MCP Catalog now includes integrations for , , and dozens more. Whatever tools your team depends on, they’re now just a command away. We will continue to expand the catalog as new remote servers become available.

Figure 1: Some examples of remote MCP servers that are now part of the Docker MCP Catalog

Easy to use via the CLI or Docker Desktop 

No new setup. No steep learning curve. Just use your existing Docker CLI and get going. Enabling and authorizing remote MCP servers is fully integrated into the familiar command-line experience you already love. You can also install servers via one-click with Docker Desktop.

Two Commands to Connect and Authorize Remote MCP Servers- It’s That Simple

Step 1: Enable Your Remote MCP Server

Pick your server, and enable it with one line:

docker mcp server enable notion-remote

This registers the remote server and prepares it for OAuth authorization.

Step 2: Authorize Securely with OAuth

Next, authorize your connection with:

docker mcp oauth authorize notion-remote

This launches your browser with an OAuth authorization page.

Step 1: Enable Your Remote MCP Server

If you prefer to use Docker Desktop instead of the command line, open the Catalog tab and search for the server you want to use. The cloud icon indicates that it’s a remote server. Click the “+” button to enable the server.

Figure 2: Enabling the Linear remote MCP server is just one click.

Step 2: Authorize Securely with OAuth

Open the OAuth tab and click the “Authorize” button next to the MCP Server you want to authenticate with.

Figure 3: Built-in OAuth flow for Linear remote MCP servers. 

Once authorized, your connection is live. You can now interact with Notion, Linear, or any other supported MCP server directly through your Docker MCP environment.

Why This Update Matters for Developers

Unified Access Across Your Ecosystem

Developers rely on dozens of tools every day across different MCP clients. The Docker MCP Toolkit brings them together under one secure, unified interface – helping you move faster without manually configuring each MCP client. This means you don’t need to log in to the same service multiple times across Cursor, Claude Code, and other clients you may use.

Unlock AI-Powered Workflows

Remote MCP servers make it really easy to bridge data, tools, and AI. They are always up to date with the latest tools and are faster to use as they don’t run any code on your computer. With OAuth support, your connected apps can now securely provide context to AI models unlocking powerful new automation possibilities.

Building the Future of Developer Productivity

This update is more than just an integration boost – it’s the foundation for a more connected, intelligent, and automated developer experience. And this is only the beginning.

The addition of OAuth for remote MCP servers makes Docker MCP Toolkit the most powerful way to securely connect your tools, workflows, and AI-powered automations.

With 60+ new remote servers now available and growing, developers can bring their favorite services – like Notion and Linear, directly into Docker MCP Toolkit.
